subject: english
learning_objectives: Distinguish one-sided from two-sided argumentative essays
Plan and write a persuasive argumentative essay

sections.heading: Definition
sections.content: An argumentative essay is one that requires the writer to present a subject with the aim of persuading the reader to agree with a particular point of view against another.

sections.heading: Scope and Method
sections.content:
Begin by giving a clear, intelligent definition of the subject so your reader knows exactly what is being discussed. Then proceed to give points to support your view.

Variant question types:
(i) 'Write an essay either in favour of or against…' – choose one side.
(ii) 'Write an essay both for and against…' – cover both sides.
(iii) 'Write an essay to justify the view that…' – argue the stated position only.

sections.heading: Sample Essay Framework – Co-education
sections.content:
Topic: 'Co-education should be encouraged in secondary schools.'

Introduction: Define co-education; state your position.
Point 1 (for): It prepares students for real-world interaction between genders.
Point 2 (for): Promotes equality and removes gender stereotypes.
Point 3 (against, if two-sided): Can lead to distraction.
Point 4 (against, if two-sided): Some students perform better in single-sex schools.
Conclusion: Restate your position strongly.
